questions.question: Explain the Church-Turing Thesis and discuss its significance in the context of computability theory. Why is it considered a thesis rather than a theorem?
keyPoints: Church-Turing Thesis states that any computable function can be computed by a Turing machine
It establishes equivalence between Turing-computability and the intuitive notion of computability
It is a thesis because it cannot be formally proven as it relates intuitive concept to formal definition
Provides foundation for understanding limits of what computers can do

questions.question: Discuss the historical development of the Church-Turing Thesis, mentioning the contributions of Alonzo Church and Alan Turing. How did their different approaches converge to the same conclusion?
keyPoints: Church developed lambda calculus in 1936 as formalization of computability
Turing developed Turing machine in 1936 as alternative formalization
Church's thesis: lambda-definable functions are exactly the computable functions
Turing's thesis: computable functions are exactly those solvable by Turing machine
Proving equivalence of these formalisms strengthened the thesis

questions.question: Provide a detailed proof of the equivalence between Turing machines and lambda calculus. Discuss how each formalism can simulate the other, thereby establishing that the class of computable functions is the same under both definitions.
keyPoints: Show how to simulate Turing machine computation using lambda calculus (encoding tape, transitions as lambda terms)
Show how lambda calculus can be simulated by Turing machine (convert lambda terms to Church encodings)
Reference Gödel's recursive functions as third equivalent formalism
Discuss significance: any function computable in one system is computable in others

questions.question: Explain the implications of the Church-Turing Thesis for the Halting Problem. How does this thesis help demonstrate the existence of undecidable problems? Provide a detailed analysis with relevant examples.
keyPoints: Halting Problem asks whether a given program halts on a given input
Church-Turing Thesis implies if halting problem were decidable, we could decide any computable property
Proof by contradiction using diagonalization and universal Turing machine
Turing's 1936 paper showed no algorithm can solve halting problem, proving existence of undecidable problems
Discuss broader implications: Rice's theorem, Gödel's incompleteness theorem connection
